source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 15.3.4 Sticky BIT (pp. 295-297)

questions.q: What does the sticky bit (SBIT) do, and where is it valid?
options: Only on directories: users with w can delete only their OWN files, not files created by others
On executables: it runs them as root
On directories: it hides the contents
questions.why: On a shared writable directory, the sticky bit stops users deleting each other's files - each can only delete their own (root is exempt).

questions.q: Which leading octal digit represents the sticky bit?
Add the sticky bit to the directory /share (symbolic form).
questions.why: chmod o+t sets the sticky bit; numerically the leading digit is 1 (e.g. chmod 1777).

questions.q: Which well-known directory uses the sticky bit by default (shown as drwxrwxrwt)?
questions.why: /tmp is 1777 (the trailing 't') so users can create files there but only remove their own.

questions.q: Is the root user restricted by SUID/SGID/sticky-bit rules?
options: No - root (uid=0) is not restricted by these special permissions
Yes - root obeys the sticky bit like everyone
questions.why: root can delete any file regardless of the sticky bit and is not constrained by SUID/SGID/SBIT.
